rationale_for_baseline_tier: Pityriasis rosea is a common, benign, non-contagious, self-limiting post-viral papulosquamous eruption. The patient is systemically well and afebrile with the source's own red-flag screen negative (palms/soles/face spared, no mucosal patches, no generalised lymphadenopathy, no new medications, no blistering/peeling; Risk Level: Low), so this is neither an emergency (not T4/T5) nor an urgent same-day problem (not T3). It is telehealth/GP-manageable with firm reassurance and over-the-counter symptomatic measures (a mild topical corticosteroid to the itchiest spots, an oral antihistamine, lukewarm showers, avoiding heat/harsh soaps/heavy sweating) — patient-performable self-care with a routine review pathway (the source's disposition was routine GP review in 2-3 weeks). So the baseline is self-care with routine GP review (T1) rather than an urgent tier, with prominent mandatory escalation edges for the secondary-syphilis and severe-drug-reaction / systemic-illness mimics that telehealth cannot examine or serologically test for. Reflexive escalation to 000/ED here is over-triage.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New spots appearing on the palms of the hands or the soles of the feet, sores or patches in the mouth or on the genitals, or new swollen glands
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Book a prompt GP review within a few days for examination and a blood test (syphilis serology)
escalation_edges.rationale: Palm/sole or mucosal involvement or lymphadenopathy in a papulosquamous eruption raises secondary syphilis — the great imitator — which needs serology (RPR/TPPA) that telehealth cannot perform.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the rash spreads to your palms or the soles of your feet, or you get sores in your mouth or on your genitals, or swollen glands, book a GP appointment in the next few days — you'll need an examination and a simple blood test to rule out other causes.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Feeling generally unwell with a high fever, or the rash rapidly worsening with systemic symptoms
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Seek same-day medical assessment; go to an emergency department (call 000 if rapidly and severely unwell)
escalation_edges.rationale: A high fever or systemic illness on top of a widespread eruption points away from benign pityriasis rosea toward a systemic illness or a severe cutaneous reaction needing urgent in-person assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you develop a high fever or start feeling really unwell in yourself, get seen the same day — go to an emergency department, and call 000 if you become severely unwell quickly.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Any blistering of the spots, skin starting to peel/shed, the rash becoming painful rather than itchy, or sores developing in the mouth, eyes or genitals
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Seek same-day emergency assessment (call 000 if rapidly unwell)
escalation_edges.rationale: Blistering, skin peeling, skin pain and mucosal involvement are alarm features for a severe cutaneous adverse drug reaction (SJS/TEN), a dermatological emergency needing urgent in-person management, that telehealth cannot examine for.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If any spots blister, your skin starts to peel, the rash becomes painful rather than itchy, or you get sores in your mouth, eyes or genitals, treat it as an emergency and get seen the same day — call 000 if you feel very unwell.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Baseline: systemically-well benign pityriasis rosea with a negative red-flag screen
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Start reassurance and symptomatic measures now, and arrange routine GP review within about 2-3 weeks (sooner if a red flag develops)
escalation_edges.rationale: The proportionate disposition once the syphilis, severe-drug-reaction and systemic mimics are excluded — reassure, begin patient-performable symptomatic care immediately, and arrange routine review, without urgent escalation.
escalation_edges.communication_template: This is a benign, non-contagious rash that will settle on its own over about 6-8 weeks. Use a mild steroid cream on the itchiest spots and an antihistamine for the itch, take lukewarm showers, and book a routine GP review in the next 2-3 weeks to confirm it — sooner if any of the warning signs appear.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The rash not resolving well beyond the expected 6-8 weeks, or the diagnosis feeling uncertain/atypical
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Prompt GP review if not resolving as expected or atypical
escalation_edges.rationale: A pityriasis-rosea-like eruption that persists beyond the usual course or is atypical warrants GP reassessment and consideration of syphilis serology or an alternative diagnosis.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If it hasn't largely cleared by around 8-10 weeks, or it doesn't behave the way we've described, book a GP review so they can re-examine it and consider a blood test.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Severe health anxiety about contagion and a possible severe allergic/drug reaction, triggered by the explosive onset and extent of the rash
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lead with clear, firm reassurance that the rash is benign, non-contagious and self-limiting, and directly address the fears about contagion and drug reaction; do not let the degree of distress drive over-escalation of a systemically-well benign eruption, but do confirm the patient understands the specific red flags that warrant review.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This looks like pityriasis rosea — a common, benign rash your body produces after a mild viral illness. It is not an allergy, it is not dangerous, and it is not contagious, so you don't need to avoid other people. It does take a while — usually about 6 to 8 weeks — to clear on its own. Right now: put a mild steroid cream (hydrocortisone 1%) on the itchiest spots twice a day, take an antihistamine for the itch, have cool or lukewarm showers, and avoid hot water, harsh soaps and heavy sweating. Book a routine GP review in the next 2-3 weeks to confirm it. You do not need an emergency department for the rash itself.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Routine GP review within ~2-3 weeks (own GP or a telehealth GP); 13 HEALTH (13 43 25 84) is available for advice
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If this is after hours you do not need an emergency department for the rash itself — start the symptomatic measures and reassurance and arrange a routine GP review in the next 2-3 weeks. Only seek same-day/emergency care if a red flag develops: spread to the palms/soles or sores in the mouth/genitals (prompt GP review + blood test), a high fever or feeling generally unwell, or any blistering, skin peeling or painful skin (same-day/emergency).
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Tell the reviewing GP the eruption began with a single ~4 cm oval herald patch on the left flank about 10 days after a mild viral prodrome (fatigue, sore throat), followed 24-48 hours ago by a widespread crop of 1-2 cm oval salmon-coloured papules/plaques with a fine inward-facing collarette of scale along the cleavage lines ('Christmas tree'), with clear face/palms/soles, no mucosal involvement, no lymphadenopathy, no new medications, and no fever or systemic upset — a classic pityriasis rosea; syphilis serology reserved for atypical or palm/sole-involving features.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether the widespread papulosquamous eruption is a benign pityriasis rosea or one of the mimics it must be screened against — secondary syphilis (which telehealth cannot serologically exclude) or a severe cutaneous adverse drug reaction / systemic illness — which telehealth cannot examine for.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Actively screen the palms/soles, mucosa, lymph nodes, medication history and for systemic/blistering features; treat a negative screen plus the classic herald-patch/collarette/cleavage-line picture as a reason for reassurance, self-care and routine review with explicit safety-netting — NOT as a reason to default to same-day ED / 000 when the patient is systemically well.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either dismissing a widespread eruption with pure reassurance and no safety-net for the syphilis or severe-drug-reaction mimics (under-action), or escalating a systemically-well patient with a negative red-flag screen straight to the emergency department / 000 (over-escalation).

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing a widespread papulosquamous eruption as trivial with pure reassurance and no safety-net for secondary syphilis or a severe cutaneous adverse drug reaction / systemic illness.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An unrecognised secondary syphilis goes untreated and transmissible, or an evolving severe cutaneous drug reaction progresses, because no one advised when to escalate or arrange serology.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Providing reassurance and self-care but with no mimic safety-net at all (baseline self-care is correct ONLY when paired with the mandatory red-flag escalation edges for syphilis and severe drug reaction).

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending a systemically-well patient with a negative red-flag screen to the emergency department or calling 000 for a benign, self-limiting pityriasis rosea — unnecessary ED load and patient distress/cost when reassurance with routine review is proportionate (a prompt T2 review to arrange syphilis serology is an acceptable more-cautious disposition, not scored as over-triage).
